My research investigates how generative artificial intelligence reshapes normative behaviors, creative production, and labor market dynamics in the digital economy. I examine the intersection of AI technologies, platform governance, and creative labor through a lens grounded in information systems, economics, and copyright law. I draw on emerging copyright litigation involving generative AI firms to motivate my research in understanding the legal and economic tensions between AI training data practices and creator rights. These cases provide critical insights into the structural challenges facing creative workers and the policy interventions needed to address them.

My methodological approach combines causal inference with large-scale analysis of creative artifacts, measuring supply and demand responses to AI-generated media, and of existing and emerging policy frameworks in the creative economy. I aim to produce findings that inform both academic debate and practical policy design.
